The measurement scales will be adapted from validated technostress creator/inhibitor instruments and integrated with scales for dark side perceptions developed from prior literature on AI and deepfakes. The analysis will include tests for common method bias, reliability, and validity.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a theoretically grounded, empirical model linking established IS constructs (technostress) to the emerging challenges of IAAAM, offering a nuanced understanding of their human costs. It will contribute actionable insights for organizational practice by identifying key stress levers to manage and inhibitors to promote, thereby informing the design of more human-centric implementation strategies for AI and metaverse technologies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Dwivedi et al. has identified opportunities and challenges of generative AI and deepfakes, this study uniquely integrates the mature technostress paradigm from IS to systematically explain the formation of dark side perceptions, moving beyond descriptive lists to a testable causal model for next-generation technologies.

proposals.title: A Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) Framework for Evaluating Pragmatic Trade-offs in Generative AI and Metaverse Adoption for Supply Chain Services
proposals.motivation: The rapid emergence of technologies like generative conversational AI (e.g., ChatGPT) and the metaverse presents complex adoption dilemmas for logistics and supply chain management (SCM). Pre-2026 discourse highlights their potential for service innovation but also reveals significant challenges, including ethical risks, deepfake deceptions, and implementation costs. Decision-makers lack a structured, holistic framework to formally evaluate the multi-faceted trade-offs between benefits (efficiency, customer experience) and drawbacks (security, trust, resource allocation).
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a formal Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) framework will systematically reveal and quantify the non-obvious, pragmatic trade-offs in adopting generative AI and metaverse technologies within SCM services, leading to more robust and justifiable adoption strategies than intuitive or single-criterion evaluations.
proposals.method: The research will employ a hybrid MCDA methodology. First, a Delphi study with SCM and AI experts will identify and weight key criteria (e.g., cost, operational efficiency, data privacy, ethical risk, user trust) derived from pre-2026 literature. Second, we will apply the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) to structure the decision problem hierarchically and derive priority weights. Third, the Technique for Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS) will be used to rank hypothetical or real-world adoption scenarios for specific SCM services (e.g., AI-powered customer service chatbots, metaverse-based warehouse simulations). Sensitivity analysis will validate the robustness of the rankings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, actionable decision-support tool for managers evaluating disruptive digital technologies. It will contribute to theory by formally integrating the multidisciplinary perspectives on AI opportunities/challenges into a structured SCM decision model. The framework will advance the research roadmap for AI in logistics by offering a method to navigate implementation trade-offs.
proposals.novelty: While MCDA is known in SCM, its application to the pragmatic, multi-stakeholder trade-offs of nascent generative AI and metaverse technologies is novel. It moves beyond descriptive analysis of opportunities/challenges to a prescriptive, quantifiable evaluation model that bridges Dwivedi's work on AI implications, deepfakes, and SCM research.

proposals.title: Nudging the Digital Green Shift: A Behavioral Economics Framework for Promoting Climate-Friendly User Adoption in AI and Metaverse Services
proposals.motivation: The rapid proliferation of energy-intensive digital services, including generative AI (e.g., ChatGPT) and metaverse platforms, significantly contributes to the ICT sector's carbon footprint. While technical efficiency gains are pursued, user behavior remains a critical, under-addressed lever for reducing digital carbon emissions. This research addresses the urgent need to translate climate awareness into consistent, sustainable digital practices among consumers and professionals.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying targeted behavioral nudges, grounded in principles like defaults, social proof, and immediate feedback, within digital service interfaces will significantly increase user adoption of climate-friendly options (e.g., low-data streaming modes, delayed non-urgent AI processing, virtual over physical meetings) without compromising core service utility.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-methods design. First, a systematic literature review will map behavioral economics principles to digital service contexts. Second, we will develop and test prototype nudge architectures (e.g., smart default settings, carbon footprint feedback dashboards, social norm messaging) within simulated logistics management dashboards and metaverse collaboration platforms. A/B testing and controlled experiments will measure the efficacy of different nudge types on user behavior change, utilizing pre-2026 analytics and survey tools for data collection and statistical analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, empirically tested framework for 'green nudging' in digital service design, extending behavioral science into the sustainability of AI and metaverse research. It will provide actionable guidelines for service providers and policymakers to architect user choices that align individual digital behaviors with broader climate goals, complementing technical supply-chain optimizations.
proposals.novelty: While nudges are used in energy conservation, their systematic application to climate-friendly behaviors within complex, emerging digital services like generative AI and the metaverse is novel. It uniquely bridges Dwivedi's expertise in AI/technology adoption, supply chain services, and behavioral implications (from deepfakes research) with climate action.

proposals.title: Beyond Rationality: Modeling Social Mimicry and Hype in AI Adoption Decisions within Supply Chain and Service Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Dominant technology adoption models, such as TAM and UTAUT, are predicated on rational evaluation of perceived usefulness and ease of use. However, the rapid, widespread adoption of technologies like ChatGPT and the fervent investment in the metaverse suggest that social forces like mimicry, bandwagon effects, and media hype are powerful, non-rational drivers. This gap limits our understanding of real-world AI adoption, especially in complex, inter-organizational contexts like supply chains and digital services.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in organizational settings, the influence of social mimicry (e.g., competitor adoption) and industry-specific hype (amplified by media and events) on AI adoption intention is statistically significant and often outweighs the influence of traditional rational evaluations of perceived usefulness.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods design. First, a quantitative study will survey supply chain and service managers, using structural equation modeling (SEM) to test an extended UTAUT2 model incorporating constructs for social mimicry (operationalized via institutional theory) and perceived hype (measured via sentiment analysis of industry news). Second, a qualitative case study analysis of AI adoption narratives in logistics firms will be conducted using thematic analysis to uncover the mechanisms of mimicry and hype.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, integrated theoretical framework that relaxes the rational evaluation assumption, providing a more accurate model of contemporary AI adoption. It will offer practical insights for managers and policymakers on navigating hype-driven technology landscapes, aligning with Dwivedi's focus on the implications of generative AI and deepfakes for practice and policy.
proposals.novelty: While mimicry and hype are acknowledged anecdotally, this study novelly quantifies their impact relative to core rational constructs within established adoption theory, specifically within the high-stakes, networked contexts of supply chain and service management where Dwivedi's expertise lies.

proposals.title: A Cross-National Comparative Study of Generative AI Adoption Factors in Supply Chain Services: A UTAUT2-Based Analysis
proposals.motivation: The rapid emergence of generative conversational AI (e.g., ChatGPT) presents significant potential for transforming service operations and supply chain management. However, pre-2026 research, including Dwivedi et al.'s (2023) opinion paper, highlights that adoption factors are likely moderated by national and cultural contexts, creating a critical knowledge gap. Understanding these variations is essential for developing effective, context-sensitive implementation strategies and policies for global enterprises.
proposals.hypothesis: The influence of key Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology 2 (UTAUT2) constructs (e.g., performance expectancy, social influence, facilitating conditions) on the behavioral intention to adopt generative AI in supply chain services will vary significantly across national contexts, with cultural dimensions (e.g., Hofstede's indices) acting as key moderators.
proposals.method: This study employs a quantitative, survey-based methodology. We will adapt and validate a UTAUT2 instrument, tailored for generative AI in logistics/SCM contexts, and administer it to professionals in three distinct national settings (e.g., UK, India, and a GCC country). Data analysis will utilize Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) using SmartPLS software to test the core model. Multi-group analysis (MGA) within PLS-SEM will be the primary technique to statistically compare the path coefficients and moderating effects of national culture across the sampled countries.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a nuanced, empirically grounded understanding of how national culture shapes generative AI adoption in a critical business domain. It will extend the theoretical application of UTAUT2 in cross-cultural AI research and offer practical, localized insights for managers and policymakers aiming to deploy these technologies effectively in different regions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by the researcher has identified opportunities and challenges for generative AI, this study moves beyond a generic perspective to conduct a rigorous, theory-driven comparative analysis of adoption drivers specifically within supply chain services across diverse national ecosystems, a focused empirical gap in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Hybrid UTAUT-TOE Framework for Explaining Generative AI Adoption in Supply Chain Services: A Behavioral and Organizational Analysis
proposals.motivation: The rapid emergence of generative AI (e.g., ChatGPT) presents significant potential for transforming supply chain and service operations, yet its adoption remains complex and poorly understood. Existing models like TAM-TOE, while foundational, often lack the granular behavioral constructs needed to explain the nuanced decisions of individuals and firms in high-stakes, multi-actor environments like logistics. A more integrated theoretical lens is urgently needed to guide research, practice, and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating key constructs from the Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT)—specifically performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, and facilitating conditions—into the Technology-Organization-Environment (TOE) framework will provide a significantly more robust model for explaining and predicting the adoption of generative AI tools in supply chain management contexts.
proposals.method: The research will employ a quantitative, survey-based approach. A structural equation modeling (SEM) technique, specifically Partial Least Squares SEM (PLS-SEM), will be used to test the proposed hybrid framework. Data will be collected from a stratified sample of supply chain and logistics professionals. The analysis will involve validating measurement models, testing the structural model for hypothesized relationships between UTAUT constructs (integrated within the TOE's organizational context) and behavioral intention/use behavior, and comparing the explanatory power (R²) of the hybrid model against a baseline TAM-TOE model.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, validated theoretical framework that bridges individual behavioral psychology and firm-level contextual factors. It will provide empirical evidence on the most salient drivers and barriers of generative AI adoption in operational settings, offering a concrete roadmap for managers. The findings will extend Dwivedi's prior work on AI in logistics and conversational AI by providing a more nuanced, behaviorally-grounded explanatory model.
proposals.novelty: While TAM-TOE integrations are common, the specific fusion of the more comprehensive UTAUT constructs within the TOE framework to study generative AI in supply chains is novel. It moves beyond simple 'ease of use' and 'usefulness' to incorporate social dynamics and organizational support structures, directly addressing the collaborative and institutional complexities highlighted in recent work on AI and deepfakes.

proposals.title: A Cross-Industry and Cross-Cultural Analysis of Generative AI Adoption in Operations and Supply Chain Management: A Comparative Study of Manufacturing and Service Sectors
proposals.motivation: Prior research on AI adoption, including generative conversational AI like ChatGPT, has been largely siloed within specific sectors (e.g., service firms) or single-country contexts (e.g., India). This limits the generalizability of findings and understanding of how technological potential and challenges vary across different operational environments and cultural settings. A comparative study is urgently needed to inform tailored strategies for research, practice, and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: The perceived opportunities, challenges, and implementation pathways for generative AI in operations and supply chain management differ significantly between manufacturing and service sectors and are moderated by national cultural dimensions (e.g., uncertainty avoidance, power distance).
proposals.method: The study will employ a mixed-methods, multi-stage design. First, a quantitative survey, building on established technology adoption models (e.g., UTAUT), will be administered to operations and SCM professionals in manufacturing (e.g., automotive, electronics) and service (e.g., logistics, retail) firms across at least three culturally distinct countries (e.g., Germany, Japan, USA). Second, structural equation modeling (SEM) will be used to test the hypothesized relationships. Third, follow-up qualitative interviews will provide nuanced explanations for the quantitative findings.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated, cross-contextual framework for understanding generative AI adoption in operations, moving beyond sector-specific anecdotes. It will offer empirically grounded guidance for managers on contextualizing AI implementation strategies and for policymakers on designing supportive, culturally sensitive regulations.
proposals.novelty: It directly extends Dwivedi et al.'s (2023) multidisciplinary perspective on ChatGPT by applying a rigorous comparative lens across industries and cultures, a gap noted in prior AI-in-logistics roadmaps. It integrates supply chain management and cross-cultural management theories to explain variance in AI adoption.

proposals.title: The Asymmetry of Ad Value: A Prospect Theory Investigation of Negative Consumer Responses in AI-Mediated Service Encounters
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of AI-driven conversational agents (e.g., ChatGPT) and immersive platforms (e.g., the metaverse) is transforming service delivery and advertising. Pre-2026 research on advertising value focuses on its positive effects, but the potential for negative reactions to low-value, intrusive ads in these new contexts is under-explored. This gap is critical for supply chain and service managers deploying AI interfaces, as negative user experiences can directly impact adoption and brand perception.
proposals.hypothesis: In AI-mediated service encounters, the negative impact of low-advertising-value stimuli on user satisfaction and continuance intention is significantly stronger than the positive impact of high-advertising-value stimuli. This asymmetry is amplified in contexts of high cognitive load or goal-directed user tasks.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods design. First, a controlled experiment within a simulated metaverse retail environment will expose participants to AI-generated service interactions with varying ad value (manipulated via relevance, entertainment, and informativeness). User responses (EEG, eye-tracking, self-report) will be collected. Second, we will analyze real-world user interaction logs from a chatbot service platform using natural language processing (specifically, sentiment analysis via BERT and topic modeling) to correlate ad exposure features with session abandonment and negative feedback. The data will be analyzed using structural equation modeling with a focus on comparing effect sizes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, theory-driven framework for understanding the asymmetric risks of AI-integrated advertising, moving beyond a linear value model. It will offer actionable insights for service and supply chain managers on mitigating negative user experiences in AI-driven customer touchpoints, contributing to the responsible implementation of generative AI in business.
proposals.novelty: It applies behavioral economics (Prospect Theory) to the domain of AI-mediated advertising value, challenging the linear assumption that 'more value is better.' It uniquely integrates psychophysiological measures with log data from real AI service platforms to test this asymmetry.

proposals.title: Modeling the Social Diffusion of Purchase Intention: An Agent-Based Simulation of SMS Advertising Cascades
proposals.motivation: SMS advertising remains a potent direct marketing tool, but its true impact extends beyond the initial recipient through social influence. Pre-2026 literature on advertising effectiveness often overlooks this network-mediated diffusion, treating purchase intention as an individual outcome. Understanding this social multiplier effect is crucial for optimizing marketing resource allocation and predicting market response in an increasingly connected digital economy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the network structure (e.g., small-world vs. scale-free) and agent heterogeneity in susceptibility to influence will significantly amplify the final purchase intention rate beyond the direct exposure effect of an SMS campaign, and that this amplification follows predictable, non-linear patterns.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using a framework like NetLogo or Mesa. Agents will possess attributes (e.g., susceptibility, persuasiveness) and be connected in synthetic networks replicating real-world social structures. The simulation will initiate with a subset of agents receiving an 'SMS ad exposure' event, altering their purchase intention state based on a stochastic function. Subsequently, agents will interact with neighbors via a threshold model (e.g., Bass diffusion or a custom social influence rule) to propagate intention. We will employ Monte Carlo methods and sensitivity analysis to test robustness.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel computational framework to quantify the social ripple effect of direct marketing, moving beyond individual-level analysis. It will provide marketers and supply chain planners with insights into how network topology affects campaign velocity and saturation, aiding in forecasting demand surges. The work bridges Dwivedi's interests in AI/technology implications and supply chain management.
proposals.novelty: While ABM is used in innovation diffusion, its application to model the specific, immediate social cascades triggered by a personalized SMS ad exposure is novel. The integration of agent heterogeneity with realistic social network models to dissect purchase intention propagation addresses a gap in pre-2026 marketing and logistics literature.

proposals.title: Quantifying Consumer Trade-offs in SMS Advertising: A Discrete Choice Experiment on Privacy, Personalization, and Incentives
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of SMS-based marketing presents a critical tension for businesses and consumers, balancing personalization and effectiveness against growing privacy concerns and message fatigue. Pre-2026, while AI and data analytics drive hyper-targeted campaigns, there is a lack of empirical, quantitative understanding of how consumers value and trade-off specific SMS ad attributes, leading to suboptimal strategies and potential consumer backlash. This gap is particularly acute in supply chain and service contexts where direct-to-consumer communication is vital.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that consumer purchase intention for SMS ads is not solely driven by discount value, but by a quantifiable utility function that includes negative weights for privacy intrusion and message frequency, and positive weights for perceived relevance and timing control.
proposals.method: We will employ a state-of-the-art Discrete Choice Experiment (DCE) design. Respondents will be presented with a series of choice sets containing 2-3 hypothetical SMS ad profiles defined by attributes (e.g., discount level, use of personal data, sender identity, opt-out ease, time of delivery). Data will be analyzed using Mixed Logit (MXL) models via Hierarchical Bayes estimation to capture preference heterogeneity. The design will be optimized using D-efficient algorithms in software like Ngene, and the survey will be administered to a representative panel.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorous, utility-based model of SMS ad effectiveness, moving beyond descriptive studies to quantify the marginal value of different ad features. It will offer actionable insights for marketing and supply chain managers to design communication strategies that maximize conversion while respecting consumer preferences. The findings will also inform policy discussions on digital advertising by empirically highlighting privacy trade-offs.
proposals.novelty: While DCE is established in economics, its application to model the micro-attributes of SMS advertising, integrating constructs from AI-driven personalization and deepfake/deception research (e.g., sender authenticity), is novel. It bridges Dwivedi's work on AI implications and supply chain communication with robust econometric methods rarely applied in this domain.

proposals.title: Micro-Moment Persuasion: A Field Experiment on the Efficacy of AI-Generated, Contextual SMS Ads for Immediate Conversion vs. Long-Term Brand Impact
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of conversational AI (e.g., ChatGPT) enables hyper-personalized, real-time marketing communications at scale. However, the impact of such single, context-triggered micro-interventions on consumer behavior is poorly understood, especially when balancing immediate sales against long-term brand health. This gap is critical for supply chain and service management, where real-time demand shaping and customer relationship management intersect.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AI-generated, highly contextual SMS ads will significantly increase immediate micro-conversions (e.g., click, app open) but may have a neutral or negative effect on long-term purchase intention and brand perception compared to less frequent, brand-aligned messaging.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, randomized field experiment in partnership with a retail service provider. A two-stage AI pipeline will be deployed: first, a transformer-based model (e.g., fine-tuned GPT-3/4 or open-source alternative like Llama 2) will generate ad copy using real-time contextual triggers (location, time, weather). Second, a reinforcement learning bandit algorithm (e.g., Thompson Sampling) will optimize send-time and copy variants for immediate conversion. We will measure immediate behavior via digital tracking and long-term effects through follow-up surveys and subsequent purchase data over a 90-day period.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, behavioral evidence base for the real-world efficacy of AI-driven micro-moment marketing. It will contribute to theory in technology-mediated persuasion and offer practical insights for logistics and service managers on integrating generative AI into customer touchpoints without eroding long-term value.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines AI in supply chains or the ethics of generative content, this study uniquely isolates the causal effect of a *single* AI-generated message at the micro-moment level, directly contrasting short-term behavioral nudges with longitudinal brand outcomes—a tension highlighted but not empirically resolved in existing literature.

proposals.title: Generative AI for Prescriptive Marketing: An Inverse Design Framework for Optimizing SMS Ad Characteristics to Maximize Purchase Intention
proposals.motivation: Generative AI (GenAI) like ChatGPT is predominantly used descriptively to analyze or generate content. In digital marketing, especially for direct channels like SMS, there is a critical need to move beyond prediction to prescription. Pre-2026, the potential of using these models inversely to prescribe optimal message characteristics for specific consumer segments remains largely unexplored, representing a significant gap for data-driven marketing and supply chain demand generation.
proposals.hypothesis: A GenAI model, trained on historical SMS ad performance data and consumer segment profiles, can be inverted to prescribe a set of message characteristics (e.g., length, tone, keyword inclusion, call-to-action type) that will maximize the predicted purchase intention for a defined target segment.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage technical approach. First, a predictive model (e.g., a fine-tuned transformer like BERT or a structured deep neural network) will be trained to predict purchase intention from SMS ad features and consumer segment variables. Second, we will treat this trained model as a differentiable simulator and use gradient-based optimization techniques (e.g., Projected Gradient Descent) or reinforcement learning (e.g., a policy network) to solve the inverse problem: finding the input ad features that maximize the model's output for a given segment vector, subject to practical constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel prescriptive framework for AI in marketing, shifting the focus from analysis to actionable design. It provides a methodological blueprint for the inverse use of GenAI in business contexts. The findings will offer concrete guidance for marketers and supply chain managers on optimizing direct communication for demand generation.
proposals.novelty: While deepfakes and conversational AI demonstrate GenAI's generative capability, applying it to solve a constrained business optimization problem as an inverse design task is novel. This moves beyond using AI for content creation to using it as an engine for strategic prescription, aligning with emerging 'AI for management' themes.

proposals.title: Beyond Awareness: Modeling the Dominance of Cost and Interoperability Barriers in Blockchain Adoption for Supply Chain Services
proposals.motivation: By the mid-2020s, awareness of blockchain's potential for enhancing transparency, security, and traceability in supply chains is widespread. However, adoption lags, suggesting that post-awareness barriers are now the critical impediments. This research addresses a pivotal gap in technology adoption literature by shifting focus from knowledge deficits to the tangible economic and technical hurdles that dominate decision-making when potential benefits are already understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in contexts of high blockchain awareness, perceived adoption intention is inversely and more strongly correlated with perceived cost and interoperability barriers than with perceived benefits. Furthermore, we posit that the moderating effect of organizational size and supply chain complexity on this relationship is significant.
proposals.method: The study will employ a mixed-methods design. First, a quantitative survey of logistics and supply chain managers will measure constructs (perceived benefits, cost barriers, interoperability barriers, adoption intention) using validated scales. Data will be analyzed using Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) to test the hypothesized relationships. Second, qualitative semi-structured interviews will provide nuanced insights into the specific nature of cost and interoperability challenges, grounding the statistical model in managerial reality.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, barrier-centric model of technology adoption for blockchain in supply chains, extending established theories like TOE and DOI. It will provide practitioners and policymakers with evidence-based guidance on which specific barriers to target (e.g., integration costs vs. protocol standardization) to accelerate adoption. The findings will also inform the design of more effective blockchain-as-a-service (BaaS) offerings and interoperability frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While prior research often treats low awareness as the primary adoption hurdle, this study uniquely assumes high awareness as its starting point, a novel and increasingly relevant context. It synthesizes Dwivedi's expertise in AI/technology adoption with supply chain and service research to investigate the specific, post-hype friction points that determine real-world implementation.

proposals.title: Mapping the Facilitator Nexus: A Systems Dynamics and Interpretive Structural Modeling Approach to AI Adoption in Supply Chain and Service Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Prior research on AI adoption, especially for transformative technologies like generative AI and the metaverse in supply chain and service contexts, has predominantly focused on identifying and ranking barriers. This deficit-oriented perspective offers limited actionable guidance for practitioners seeking to enable adoption. A shift towards modeling the complex interrelationships and synergies between adoption facilitators is crucial to provide a constructive roadmap for implementation and value realization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a core set of key adoption facilitators—such as strategic alignment, data readiness, change management capability, and perceived trust/transparency—are not independent but form a hierarchical, interdependent system where certain facilitators act as foundational drivers for others.
proposals.method: The study will employ a two-phase, mixed-methods approach. First, a systematic literature review and expert interviews (with supply chain and service managers) will identify and validate a comprehensive set of facilitators. Second, we will apply Interpretive Structural Modeling (ISM) to model the contextual relationships and hierarchical structure among these facilitators. The resulting ISM digraph will be validated and dynamically simulated using a Systems Dynamics model to test the impact of strengthening foundational facilitators on the overall adoption ecosystem.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, practitioner-oriented 'facilitator nexus' framework that maps the pathways to AI adoption. It will advance theory by applying and extending systems modeling techniques (ISM, Systems Dynamics) to the positive psychology of technology adoption. For practice, it will provide managers with a prioritized intervention model to strategically enable AI integration.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the dominant barrier-centric paradigm to a proactive, enablement-focused systems model, and in the novel integration of ISM with Systems Dynamics specifically for modeling facilitator interrelationships in the AI adoption context, an approach not prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Empirical Validation of a TISM Model for AI Adoption Barriers: A Multi-Case Study in Logistics and Supply Chain Services
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, interpretive structural models like TISM (Total Interpretive Structural Modeling) are widely used to theorize complex barriers to technology adoption, such as AI in supply chains. However, these models are often conceptual and lack rigorous empirical validation, limiting their utility for practitioners and policymakers. Grounding such models in real-world evidence is crucial to move from theoretical frameworks to actionable insights for managing digital transformation.
proposals.hypothesis: The structural relationships and hierarchy of barriers proposed in a pre-validated TISM model for AI adoption will be confirmed and refined through empirical data from specific industry case studies. Contextual factors, such as industry sector and firm size, will significantly moderate the strength and salience of these barriers.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multiple-case study design in 3-4 industries (e.g., pharmaceutical logistics, automotive supply chain, third-party logistics services). Data will be collected via semi-structured interviews with managers and archival analysis of AI project documents. The core technical approach involves using pattern-matching logic to compare the empirically observed causal sequences and inter-barrier relationships against the pre-defined TISM structural model. Cross-case synthesis will be performed to identify consistent and divergent patterns, leading to a refined, empirically-grounded model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first empirical validations of a TISM model in the AI/technology adoption domain, strengthening the methodological rigor of interpretive structural research. It will yield a contextually nuanced framework of barriers that logistics and supply chain service managers can use to diagnose and mitigate AI implementation challenges more effectively.
proposals.novelty: While TISM is established for model development, its systematic empirical validation through comparative case studies is novel. This bridges a critical gap between interpretive theory-building and evidence-based validation, specifically for emerging technologies like AI in complex service systems.

proposals.title: Simulating the Diffusion of Generative AI in Supply Chain Networks: An Agent-Based Model of Barriers and Adoption Cascades
proposals.motivation: The potential of generative AI (e.g., ChatGPT) to transform supply chain services is widely acknowledged, yet its adoption by firms is uneven and hindered by complex barriers like cost, trust, and skill gaps. Understanding how these barriers interact within interconnected business networks to accelerate or stall diffusion is critical for effective policy and management, but existing research lacks dynamic, network-aware simulation tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the diffusion of generative AI in a supply chain network is non-linear and that specific, non-technical barriers (e.g., perceived reputational risk, partner dependency) will create critical percolation thresholds that can prevent widespread adoption even when the technology's utility is high.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using a framework like NetLogo or Mesa. Each firm-agent will be characterized by attributes (size, innovativeness, digital maturity) and make adoption decisions based on a cost-benefit function integrating barriers identified from pre-2026 literature. The network structure (scale-free, small-world) will represent supply chain linkages. Decision rules will incorporate social influence, herd behavior, and competitive pressure, allowing us to simulate diffusion cascades under various barrier configurations and network topologies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel simulation environment for testing 'what-if' scenarios in AI adoption policy for logistics. It will provide supply chain managers and policymakers with evidence-based insights into which barriers are most critical to address to trigger positive adoption cascades, moving beyond static survey-based studies.
proposals.novelty: While ABM is used in innovation diffusion, its application to model the adoption of generative AI—specifically within the complex, interdependent context of supply chain networks and with a focus on the nuanced barriers highlighted in recent AI ethics and deepfake research—is a novel synthesis of Dwivedi's research themes.

proposals.title: Grounding the Research Agenda in Non-Adoption: A Behavioral Taxonomy of Generative AI Rejection and Its Implications for Research Gaps
proposals.motivation: The rapid proliferation of generative AI (GenAI) like ChatGPT has sparked significant research into adoption and use, yet a critical gap exists in systematically understanding non-adoption. Pre-2026, studies on technology rejection (e.g., in IS, logistics) and emerging concerns over AI ethics and deepfakes highlight that rejector phenomena are not merely gaps in diffusion but signals of substantive research needs. A structured analysis of rejection is essential to steer the GenAI research agenda towards responsible and effective integration, addressing real-world barriers rather than assumed trajectories.
proposals.hypothesis: That the systematic classification of reasons for GenAI rejection, grounded in empirical user data, will reveal a structured taxonomy of research gaps that are distinct from and more sociotechnically critical than gaps identified from adoption-centric studies alone.
proposals.method: The research employs a mixed-methods design. First, qualitative data (interviews, focus groups) from identified GenAI non-users will be collected and analyzed using thematic analysis to identify core rejection dimensions (e.g., ethical, usability, utility). Second, these dimensions will inform the construction of a hierarchical taxonomy. Third, the taxonomy will be validated and prioritized using quantitative surveys analyzed via structural equation modeling (SEM) to assess the prevalence and interrelationships of rejection factors, translating them into concrete, actionable research gaps.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel, behaviorally-grounded framework for identifying and prioritizing research gaps in GenAI, moving beyond speculative agendas. It provides a validated taxonomy for scholars and a strategic roadmap for practitioners and policymakers, directly linking societal concerns (e.g., from deepfakes, ethical AI) to targeted research in AI design, communication, and governance.
proposals.novelty: While taxonomies of AI challenges exist, this proposal uniquely derives its structure from the empirical phenomenon of user rejection, synthesizing Dwivedi's work on AI opportunities/risks with supply chain and service management methods for studying non-adoption to create a demand-side, gap-driven research agenda.

proposals.title: Living Synthesis: A Protocol for Real-Time Micro-Reviews from Diary Studies of Generative AI Usage
proposals.motivation: The rapid, context-dependent adoption of LLMs like ChatGPT in professional and personal domains creates a critical knowledge gap. Traditional research methods struggle to capture the nuanced, real-time interaction patterns and emergent user strategies. There is an urgent need for scholarly synthesis that is both grounded in rich, in-situ qualitative data and agile enough to inform practice and policy as behaviors evolve.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured protocol for generating iterative 'micro-reviews' from longitudinal diary study data can produce timely, actionable scholarly insights into LLM usage patterns that are more contextually valid and responsive to rapid behavioral change than traditional review articles or one-off qualitative studies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-wave diary study with professionals from sectors like logistics, supply chain, and service management, capturing detailed logs of LLM interactions, prompts, and contextual factors. Using a hybrid qualitative analysis approach, we will first code data thematically (e.g., for prompt engineering, error mitigation, task integration). A structured protocol, informed by rapid review methods, will then guide the synthesis of these coded excerpts into focused micro-reviews (800-1200 words) on niche topics. This process will be supported by a version-controlled document system to manage the iterative updates as new diary entries are analyzed.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a novel methodological framework for real-time scholarly synthesis from rich qualitative data, directly addressing the pace of change in AI adoption. It will yield a corpus of living, evidence-based micro-reviews on specific LLM usage patterns, offering immediate value to researchers and practitioners in fields like information systems and supply chain management. The protocol itself contributes to methodological innovation in qualitative and review research.
proposals.novelty: While diary studies and literature reviews exist, the fusion into an iterative protocol for producing 'living' micro-reviews from ongoing in-situ data is novel. It moves beyond static snapshots to create a dynamic knowledge synthesis mechanism tailored for fast-evolving technologies.

ideas.title: Integrate additional behavioral theories into model
ideas.description: Enhance the TAM-TOE framework with constructs from UTAUT or innovation diffusion theory.
ideas.sourceTitle: Understanding AI adoption in manufacturing and production firms using an integrated TAM-TOE model

ideas.title: Dynamic Causal Modeling of AI Acceptance: A Multi-Wave Panel Study
ideas.description: This research conducts a longitudinal, multi-wave panel study where the same cohort of users is surveyed at multiple strategic intervals (e.g., initial use, after 3 months, after 6 months). At each wave, a quantitative survey rigorously tests an extended UTAUT model for ChatGPT acceptance. By applying cross-lagged panel analysis and latent growth modeling to the repeated quantitative measures, the study can statistically model the evolution of the acceptance framework itself, revealing how constructs like performance expectancy or social influence change in their relative importance and interrelationships over the adoption lifecycle.

ideas.title: Longitudinal study on usage evolution
ideas.description: Track how user acceptance factors change over time with increased experience.
ideas.sourceTitle: 'Okay google, what about my privacy?': User's privacy perceptions and acceptance of voice based digital assistants

ideas.title: Longitudinal Psychometric Profiling of Technology Acceptance Trajectories
ideas.description: This research integrates established psychometric scales (e.g., for trust propensity, tech anxiety, openness to experience) into a longitudinal panel study that tracks users from initial exposure through sustained interaction with a technology. By repeatedly measuring both the stable psychological traits and dynamic acceptance factors (like perceived usefulness and ease of use), the study can model how trait-by-experience interactions shape long-term adoption pathways. This approach moves beyond correlating traits with one-time acceptance to uncovering whether high-tech anxiety is permanently inhibitive or can be mitigated through positive experiences, and which user profiles are most susceptible to evolving their attitudes over time.

ideas.title: Invert the focus from barriers to enablers
ideas.description: Model the interrelationships of adoption facilitators instead of barriers.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to the adoption of blockchain technology in business supply chains: a total interpretive structural modelling (TISM) approach

ideas.title: The Technology Acceptance Model for Generative AI: Intent vs. Adaptation
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from pharmacology, where a well-documented gap exists between a drug's intended therapeutic use (designer's intent) and its real-world, off-label application driven by physician and patient experience (user perception). The analogous solution involves systematic post-market surveillance and real-world evidence studies to understand adoption factors. Adapted to the context of ChatGPT, the research proposes an 'AI-vigilance' framework. This framework would systematically compare OpenAI's stated design intents and recommended use cases (the 'label') against empirically observed user behaviors and acceptance factors gathered from digital traces and qualitative studies. The outcome is a model that maps the divergence, identifying key drivers of off-label adoption (e.g., unexpected utility for creative brainstorming) and critical 'side-effects' (e.g., prompt engineering for misinformation) that ultimately shape the technology's real-world scaling and societal integration.

ideas.title: Applying Diffusion of Innovations Theory to AI Rejection
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from the well-established Diffusion of Innovations theory in sociology and communication studies. The analogous problem—understanding why individuals or groups reject new technologies or practices—has been solved by applying Rogers' framework, which identifies five perceived attributes of an innovation and five adopter categories to systematically analyze resistance. The adapted idea applies this theoretical lens to ChatGPT rejection. It proposes a study that maps non-users and rejectors onto the innovation-decision process, using surveys and in-depth interviews to measure how the five key attributes (relative advantage, compatibility, complexity, trialability, observability) are perceived negatively compared to adopters. This moves the investigation from merely cataloging who rejects ChatGPT to understanding the specific psychological and social mechanisms—such as perceived incompatibility with professional identity or insufficient observable benefit—that lead to non-adoption at different stages of the decision process, providing a scalable theoretical model for analyzing AI rejection.

ideas.title: Apply behavioral nudge theory for adoption
ideas.description: Use insights from behavioral economics to nudge users toward climate-friendly digital behaviors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Climate change and COP26: Are digital technologies and information management part of the problem or the solution? An editorial reflection and call to action

ideas.title: Investigating ChatGPT's Organizational Adoption Through the Lens of Technology Diffusion in Healthcare Systems
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original investigation of UTAUT factors for ChatGPT by drawing an analogy from Healthcare Implementation Science, specifically the use of frameworks like the Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research (CFIR). The analogous problem—understanding how complex clinical innovations are adopted across hospital systems—is solved by analyzing a dynamic system of factors: innovation characteristics, the inner and outer organizational settings, the individuals involved, and the implementation process itself. Adapted to this context, the research would investigate ChatGPT's organizational adoption by constructing a multi-level model. This model would examine not only individual performance expectancy and effort expectancy (UTAUT) but also how ChatGPT's 'fit' with existing workflows, leadership engagement, implementation climate, available resources, and structured rollout processes collectively determine successful scaling from isolated team use to broad, sanctioned organizational adoption.

ideas.title: Validate TISM model with empirical case studies
ideas.description: Test the structural model of barriers through real-world case studies in specific industries.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to the adoption of blockchain technology in business supply chains: a total interpretive structural modelling (TISM) approach

ideas.title: Adapt Pharmacogenomic Interaction Analysis to UTAUT Moderation Research
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from pharmacogenomics, where a standard treatment's effect is moderated by patient genetics. The analogous solution involves stratified trials and interaction modeling to personalize medicine. Adapted to the UTAUT and ChatGPT context, the research would systematically test how gender and education moderate the relationships between core UTAUT factors (Performance Expectancy, Effort Expectancy, Social Influence, Facilitating Conditions) and behavioral intention/use of ChatGPT. By employing a multi-group SEM design on survey data, the study would move beyond simple group comparisons to formally test for moderation, potentially revealing that the strength of, for example, Social Influence on adoption intention is significantly stronger for one gender or education level, thereby enabling more nuanced, subgroup-specific adoption models and intervention strategies.

ideas.title: Compare adoption factors across country contexts

ideas.sourceTitle: Understanding AI adoption in manufacturing and production firms using an integrated TAM-TOE model

ideas.title: Scale down to specific use cases

ideas.sourceTitle: 'Okay google, what about my privacy?': User's privacy perceptions and acceptance of voice based digital assistants

ideas.title: The Interactivity Paradox: How Team-Level Perceptions of AI Chatbot Interactivity Influence Organizational Adoption
ideas.description: This research investigates the paradoxical role of perceived interactivity in the organizational adoption of generative AI tools like ChatGPT. It examines how high interactivity, while initially attractive at the individual level, can lead to team-level frustrations—such as inconsistent outputs, workflow disruption, or accountability ambiguity—that negatively shape collective performance expectancy and effort expectancy, key UTAUT constructs. The study will identify the specific conditions under which interactivity transitions from a facilitator to a inhibitor of adoption, analyzing the mediating role of team communication patterns and shared mental models. Ultimately, it aims to develop a contingent model that advises organizations on calibrating interactivity perceptions and managing implementation processes to avoid adoption pitfalls.

ideas.title: AI adoption is beneficial for all SMEs
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that AI adoption for risk management is universally positive for SMEs.

ideas.title: A Taxonomy of Generative AI Rejection: Classifying Research Gaps from Non-User Phenomena
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the study of ChatGPT rejectors with the development of a research gap taxonomy to create a new methodological framework. It investigates the reasons for non-adoption—such as ethical concerns, usability issues, or perceived lack of utility—and uses these empirical findings to construct a taxonomy of actionable research gaps specifically within the generative AI domain. This approach grounds the taxonomy in observed human behavior and societal response, ensuring it identifies gaps that are not only academically salient but critical for responsible and effective technology integration. The outcome is a prioritized research agenda directly informed by the barriers to adoption, targeting gaps in AI ethics, design, communication, and policy.

ideas.title: Investigate other moderators like gender
ideas.description: Explore if gender or education moderate the impact of UTAUT factors on ChatGPT use.
ideas.sourceTitle: 'Okay google, what about my privacy?': User's privacy perceptions and acceptance of voice based digital assistants

ideas.title: Cohort-Controlled Longitudinal Study on the Causal Effect of Experience on User Acceptance
ideas.description: This study employs a quasi-experimental, staggered rollout design to isolate the causal effect of user experience on acceptance factors. By introducing the technology to new user cohorts at different calendar times, the research can compare acceptance metrics between cohorts at equivalent experience levels (e.g., 3 months in) while controlling for period effects like market changes or software updates. This design disentangles the evolution due to learning and adaptation from confounding temporal trends. The result is a robust, causal model of how acceptance drivers shift with accumulated experience, independent of external events.

ideas.title: Grounded Theory and Scale Development for AI Assistant Acceptance Prior to Cross-Platform Model Testing
ideas.description: This research first establishes a validated, context-specific measurement model for AI assistant acceptance before any broad application. Phase 1 uses grounded theory analysis of user interviews to identify salient beliefs and behaviors, ensuring the core constructs of the extended UTAUT model are relevant and comprehensive. Phase 2 develops and psychometrically validates new survey scales based on these qualitative findings. Only then, in Phase 3, is the empirically grounded model tested quantitatively across a strategically selected set of voice and text-based AI assistants, ensuring robust and generalizable conclusions about cross-platform acceptance drivers.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: From intention to ad design
ideas.description: Use the model inversely to prescribe SMS ad characteristics that maximize purchase intention for a target segment.

ideas.title: Scale down to micro-moment interventions

ideas.sourceTitle: Does SMS advertising still have relevance to increase consumer purchase intention? A hybrid PLS-SEM-neural network modelling approach

ideas.title: Longitudinal Study of User Acceptance Evolution Using Ecological Succession Models
ideas.description: This research adapts the longitudinal study methods of ecological succession to track how user acceptance factors change over time with increased experience. In ecology, succession is studied by monitoring permanent plots to see how species communities and environmental conditions evolve through predictable stages. The analogous solution is to apply this staged, mechanistic framework to user-technology interaction. The adapted idea treats user cohorts as ecological communities and acceptance factors as the environment, using longitudinal data collection to map the 'successional stages' of acceptance (from initial adoption to deep integration) and identify the specific drivers that cause shifts from one stage to the next, moving beyond simple tracking to a model of dynamic progression.

ideas.title: Attitude linearly leads to behavioral intention

ideas.sourceTitle: Understanding managers’ attitudes and behavioral intentions towards using artificial intelligence for organizational decision-making

ideas.title: A Contingency Framework: Mapping Dimensions of Perceived Interactivity to Context-Dependent User Outcomes
ideas.description: This research develops and tests a contingency framework that deconstructs perceived interactivity into its core dimensions (e.g., user control, system responsiveness, reciprocal communication). It investigates how these distinct dimensions interact with key contextual moderators—including user characteristics (e.g., motivation, skill) and task demands (e.g., goal-directed vs. exploratory)—to produce a spectrum of outcomes ranging from engagement and satisfaction to frustration and cognitive overload. The study employs a multi-method design combining controlled experiments to isolate causal pathways and field studies to validate the framework in real-world applications, aiming to provide actionable, context-specific design principles.

ideas.title: Comparative study across industries and countries

ideas.sourceTitle: Understanding dark side of artificial intelligence (AI) integrated business analytics: assessing firm’s operational inefficiency and competitiveness

ideas.title: A Multi-Level Analysis of Generative AI Institutionalization: From Individual Use to Organizational Routines
ideas.description: This research investigates how ChatGPT transitions from sporadic individual use to a legitimized, embedded organizational routine. It employs a multi-level framework to analyze the interplay between individual acceptance factors (extended UTAUT), team-level coordination and workflow integration, and organizational-level enablers like policy, training, and leadership support. The study uses a comparative, longitudinal case study approach combined with network analysis of usage logs to map the diffusion pathways and identify critical inflection points for sustainable adoption. The outcome is a process model of generative AI institutionalization that provides actionable guidance for managing the socio-technical complexities of organizational integration.

ideas.title: Use multi-criteria decision analysis for trade-offs

ideas.sourceTitle: Climate change and COP26: Are digital technologies and information management part of the problem or the solution? An editorial reflection and call to action

ideas.sourceTitle: Does SMS advertising still have relevance to increase consumer purchase intention? A hybrid PLS-SEM-neural network modelling approach

ideas.title: Tracing the Causal Pathway: Does User Acceptance Precede or Follow Ethically Problematic LLM Use?
ideas.description: This research moves beyond correlation to investigate the causal and temporal relationship between user acceptance and ethically problematic use of ChatGPT. A longitudinal panel study measures acceptance, critical thinking dispositions, and actual use patterns at multiple points to establish if acceptance predicts subsequent ethical lapses. Concurrently, controlled experiments manipulate perceived AI reliability to test its causal effect on critical scrutiny in high-stakes scenarios. The integrated analysis identifies mediating factors (e.g., diminished vigilance) and confounding variables (e.g., expertise) to build a robust model of the acceptance-ethics link.

ideas.title: Interactivity is always perceived positively

ideas.sourceTitle: 'Okay google, what about my privacy?': User's privacy perceptions and acceptance of voice based digital assistants

ideas.title: Niche Acceptance via Domain-Specific Adaptation Protocols
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from Evolutionary Biology's Niche Theory to the problem of understanding acceptance factors for niche ChatGPT uses. In ecology, specialists succeed by adapting to specific environmental dimensions (a niche), with success measured by fit to those constraints, not universal fitness. The analogous solution involves defining key niche dimensions and mapping specific adaptations to them. Adapted to AI acceptance research, this means moving beyond general factors to define the precise 'niche' for uses like creative writing or coding by isolating their core dimensions (e.g., need for stylistic control, debugging support, integration with other tools). The study would then identify which ChatGPT features or usage patterns act as successful 'adaptations' to these dimensions, creating a model to predict and explain user acceptance based on this specialized fit, thereby scaling down the analysis to specific, bounded use-case ecologies.

ideas.title: Employ technostress models from IS research
ideas.description: Integrate specific technostress creators and inhibitors as antecedents to the 'dark side' perceptions in IAAAM.
ideas.sourceTitle: Understanding managers’ attitudes and behavioral intentions towards using artificial intelligence for organizational decision-making

ideas.title: Adapt Factorial Survey Design from Sociology to Isolate Privacy Feature Effects
ideas.description: This idea adapts the factorial survey design (FSD), a well-established vignette methodology from sociology and social psychology, to the problem of isolating the causal effects of specific privacy features on user acceptance. In sociology, FSD is used to study complex human judgments by presenting respondents with randomized, multi-attribute vignettes (e.g., profiles of job applicants with varying education, experience, and gender) to cleanly estimate the effect of each factor. The adapted solution involves constructing detailed, realistic privacy scenario vignettes where critical features—such as data use purpose, security certification, consent mechanism, and data recipient—are independently and orthogonally varied across a controlled design. By administering these to participants and collecting acceptance or trust judgments, researchers can employ linear models to precisely estimate the marginal causal effect of each privacy feature, as well as interaction effects between features, thereby moving beyond correlational findings to identify the specific drivers and deal-breakers in privacy design.

ideas.title: Inverse: How AI perceives user acceptance
ideas.description: Model how an AI system could infer user acceptance from interaction patterns.

ideas.title: Micro-Reviews of Diary Study Data for Real-Time LLM Usage Patterns
ideas.description: This research direction develops a protocol for generating concise, highly focused 'micro-review' articles that synthesize findings from diary studies of ChatGPT (or similar LLM) usage. Each micro-review addresses a very specific, niche question about user behavior, contextual factors, or interaction patterns, using the rich, real-time data from participant diaries as its primary evidence base. The process is iterative, where ongoing diary study data continuously informs and updates the micro-reviews, making them living documents that reflect evolving practices. This approach bridges the gap between deep qualitative, in-situ data and the need for timely, digestible scholarly synthesis for sub-fields like prompt engineering, domain-specific adoption, or coping with model limitations.

ideas.title: Test IAAAM model in different cultural contexts
ideas.description: Replicate the study in non-UK countries to examine cultural influences on AI acceptance-avoidance factors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Understanding managers’ attitudes and behavioral intentions towards using artificial intelligence for organizational decision-making

ideas.title: Cross-Platform Inverse Inference: AI-Driven Modeling of User Acceptance Transfer Between Assistants
ideas.description: This research develops an AI system that uses inverse modeling to infer the latent UTAUT constructs (like effort expectancy, performance expectancy) governing a user's acceptance, based solely on their interaction patterns with one AI assistant. The novel synergy is that this inferred psychological model is then applied to predict the user's likely acceptance and interaction style with a *different*, unfamiliar AI assistant (e.g., moving from a voice-based to a text-based system). This enables the study of cross-platform acceptance transfer and creates a foundation for AI assistants that can proactively adapt or make recommendations based on a portable, user-centric acceptance profile derived from behavior elsewhere.

ideas.title: A Multi-Layer Framework Disentangling Macro-Cultural and Intra-Cultural Moderators of AI Chatbot Acceptance
ideas.description: This research moves beyond national comparisons to model how both broad cultural value structures and critical within-culture individual differences jointly shape AI acceptance. It employs a stratified sampling design across selected cultural clusters to ensure representation of diverse sub-groups (e.g., youth vs. elders, tech workers vs. artisans). Using configural invariance testing in structural equation modeling, the study identifies which acceptance factors (e.g., perceived usefulness, trust, ethical concern) are universally applicable versus culturally contingent, and how their influence is moderated by individual positioning within a cultural context.

ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to the adoption of blockchain technology in business supply chains: a total interpretive structural modelling (TISM) approach

ideas.title: The Automation Bias Transfer: Measuring how user acceptance of ChatGPT reduces ethical vigilance
ideas.description: This research seed expands via analogy to the field of Aviation Human Factors, specifically the well-studied problem of 'automation bias' where high pilot trust and acceptance of flight management systems leads to complacency, reduced situation awareness, and failure to catch system errors. The analogous solution involved operationalizing and measuring specific complacency behaviors (e.g., lack of cross-checking raw data) and designing training to foster 'calibrated trust.' Adapted to the ChatGPT context, the research would investigate if high user acceptance correlates with a measurable 'AI complacency' syndrome, characterized by reduced fact-checking, lowered sensitivity to bias or harmful content, and increased delegation of ethical judgment to the AI. The study would employ methods from aviation research—such as simulated task environments with seeded errors and ethical dilemmas—to quantify this relationship and test targeted interventions designed to decouple acceptance from uncritical use.

ideas.title: Mapping the Co-evolution of User Practice and Tool Appropriation in Specialized Domains
ideas.description: This research investigates how acceptance factors for generative AI tools dynamically form and change as users integrate them into specialized professional or creative workflows. Instead of examining broad use cases, it embeds within specific communities (e.g., academic historians, mobile app developers) to conduct longitudinal studies that combine quantitative interaction data with qualitative depth. The core aim is to develop a process theory of acceptance, identifying how initial barriers are overcome, new practices emerge, and the tool's perceived value shifts as both the user and their community adapt. This reveals not just if a tool is accepted, but the contingent and evolving pathways through which it becomes woven into the fabric of specialized work.

ideas.sourceTitle: 'Okay google, what about my privacy?': User's privacy perceptions and acceptance of voice based digital assistants

ideas.title: The Interactivity Paradox: When High Control Leads to Cognitive Load and User Dissatisfaction
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that interactivity is always perceived positively by drawing an analogy from behavioral ecology's optimal foraging theory. In that field, the 'paradox of choice' demonstrates that too many interactive decision points (e.g., complex prey selection) can lead to cognitive overload and decreased net gain for an organism. The analogous solution involves modeling the cognitive costs of decision-making to find thresholds where complexity becomes detrimental. Adapted to HCI research, this idea proposes to model user satisfaction as a function of interactive complexity versus cognitive load. The study would identify specific conditions—such as task complexity, user expertise, and interface design—where high perceived interactivity triggers frustration, decision fatigue, and dissatisfaction, thereby mapping the boundary conditions of the 'interactivity positivity' assumption.

ideas.title: Cross-Platform Validation of the Extended UTAUT Model Using Comparative Pharmacokinetic Study Design
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodology of comparative pharmacokinetic studies from clinical pharmacology to validate the extended UTAUT model across diverse AI assistants. In pharmacology, a crossover design is used to test if a pharmacokinetic model for a drug holds true for different formulations (e.g., tablet vs. liquid) by administering them to the same subjects and testing for bioequivalence. The adapted idea applies this rigorous, within-subjects experimental framework to human-AI interaction research. A cohort of participants would interact with multiple, distinct AI assistants (e.g., a major voice assistant, a leading text-based LLM interface, and an emerging multimodal agent) in a randomized sequence. After each interaction with a specific assistant performing standardized tasks, the user's perceptions related to the extended UTAUT constructs would be measured. The core analysis would then statistically test for 'model equivalence' across platforms—assessing whether the structural relationships between constructs (like the effect of performance expectancy on behavioral intention) and the model's overall predictive validity remain stable. This approach moves beyond simple application of the model to new platforms and provides a formal, methodological test of its generalizability as a foundational theory for AI assistant adoption.

ideas.title: Personalized Living Reviews: Tailoring Evidence Synthesis to User Cognitive Traits
ideas.description: This research investigates the design of adaptive, 'living' systematic reviews that integrate psychometric assessments of user traits—such as need for cognitive closure, trust propensity, and tech anxiety—to personalize the evidence synthesis experience. In fast-moving fields, the system would dynamically adjust the review's complexity, visualizations, and the prominence of new versus established evidence based on the user's profile. This moves beyond a one-size-fits-all living review to create a tailored knowledge tool that mitigates user-specific barriers to understanding and adoption of rapidly evolving science.

ideas.title: Contextual & Behavioral Psychometric Modeling via Implicit In-Interaction Sensing
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond static questionnaires by fusing initial scale estimates with real-time behavioral sensing to model user traits contextually. It continuously measures implicit behavioral signals (e.g., cursor movements, dialog interaction patterns, and task completion strategies) that are diagnostic of states like tech anxiety or trust propensity within the specific interface. This creates a dynamic, validated trait model that reflects how the trait actually manifests during use, overcoming the low ecological validity of a one-time scale. The system can then adapt interfaces or support in real-time based on this contextualized model.

ideas.title: Longitudinal study of barrier evolution over time
ideas.description: Track how the influence and relationships of barriers change as technology matures.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to the adoption of blockchain technology in business supply chains: a total interpretive structural modelling (TISM) approach

ideas.title: Managers' perceptions accurately reflect system reality
ideas.description: Compare perceived opacity and risk with objective measures of system explainability and error rates.
ideas.sourceTitle: Understanding dark side of artificial intelligence (AI) integrated business analytics: assessing firm’s operational inefficiency and competitiveness

ideas.title: Cultural Mismatch in AI: Designer Intent vs. Cross-Cultural User Perception of ChatGPT
ideas.description: This research investigates the intersection of designer intent and culturally situated user acceptance of ChatGPT. It moves beyond comparing acceptance factors across cultures to critically examine how the culturally specific values and assumptions of the designers (e.g., Western notions of productivity, directness, or individualism) manifest in the AI's design and intended use cases. The study then analyzes how these embedded intents are perceived, adopted, subverted, or rejected by users in distinct cultural contexts (e.g., collectivist societies, high-context communication cultures). The outcome is a framework for identifying 'cultural mismatch' in AI systems, providing actionable insights for culturally adaptive AI design and implementation strategies.

ideas.title: Develop a taxonomy of impactful research gaps
ideas.description: Create a classification system for the types of research gaps that most effectively drive future research agendas.
ideas.sourceTitle: Writing an impactful review article: What do we know and what do we need to know?

ideas.title: Instrumented Trace Analysis: Mapping the Behavioral Delta Between Prescribed and Emergent LLM Use
ideas.description: This research implements a system to instrument ChatGPT's interface (or similar LLM front-end) to collect fine-grained behavioral traces of user interactions. These traces are analyzed against formal models of designer intent, derived from design documents and A/B test parameters, to quantify the 'behavioral delta.' The approach identifies not just perceived gaps, but the specific interaction patterns, workarounds, and tool misapplications that constitute emergent use. The output is a diagnostic framework that pinpoints where design assumptions break down and how user behavior pragmatically adapts, providing actionable evidence for redesign.

ideas.title: Study the dual: barriers to exiting blockchain
ideas.description: Investigate the challenges and lock-in effects of discontinuing a blockchain system.

ideas.title: Adapting Altmetrics and Usage-Based Frameworks from Software to Measure Review Article Impact
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from software engineering, where the impact of open-source projects is measured not by citations alone but by tangible usage metrics like downloads, forks, and dependencies. The analogous solution involves tracking these altmetrics to gauge real-world integration and utility. Adapted to the context of review articles, the research idea is to establish a parallel framework of alternative impact measures. This framework would define and operationalize metrics for teaching adoption (e.g., inclusion in syllabi), policy influence (e.g., citation in legislation or reports), clinical guideline integration, and synthesis influence. The work would involve developing methods to systematically collect and validate these metrics from diverse digital repositories, creating a more holistic picture of a review article's societal and educational impact beyond traditional academic citations.

ideas.title: Linguistic Analysis of Privacy-Neutralizing Discourse in AI Review Communities
ideas.description: This research applies computational linguistics (NLP) to analyze user-generated reviews, forum posts, and testimonials about ChatGPT and similar AI tools, specifically focusing on contexts where privacy concerns are mentioned but did not deter use. It will identify linguistic features, framing strategies, and persuasive discourse patterns (e.g., appeals to utility, trust, or social proof) that correlate with a user's decision to adopt the technology despite privacy risks. The outcome is a model of 'privacy-neutralizing language' that explains how discourse within communities can promote adoption, offering insights for both AI ethics communication and the design of transparency features that resonate with users.

ideas.title: Privacy as a Feature: Contexts Where Disclosure Enables ChatGPT Utility
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that privacy concern is always a barrier to ChatGPT use. Drawing an analogy from epidemiology, where privacy concerns in contact tracing are overcome by framing disclosure as a prosocial act for collective health, we adapt this solution to AI interaction. The study will investigate specific contexts—such as mental health support, personalized learning, or intimate creative projects—where users might be motivated to share private information with ChatGPT because the disclosure is perceived as essential to achieving a highly valued, personalized, or socially beneficial outcome. By examining how framings of 'necessary disclosure for greater good' or 'shared intimacy for personalized utility' can invert the privacy calculus, the research aims to identify conditions under which privacy concerns do not inhibit, and may even promote, deeper and more effective engagement with conversational AI.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Trait Filtering to UTAUT for AI Chatbots
ideas.description: This idea challenges the equal weighting of UTAUT factors by drawing an analogy from community ecology. In ecology, the assumption that all species traits are equally important for survival in any habitat is rejected through trait-based environmental filtering. Ecologists identify specific environmental pressures (filters) that determine which functional traits are relevant for a species to persist in a given locale. Analogously, for AI chatbots, the specific use-case context (e.g., a solitary task-oriented chatbot vs. a social companion chatbot) should act as a 'contextual filter' on the UTAUT model. This filter would selectively nullify or minimize the importance of certain factors (e.g., Social Influence might be irrelevant for a private productivity tool) while amplifying others, moving from a universally applied, equally weighted model to a contingent, context-specific framework for predicting adoption.

ideas.title: A multi-paradigm scaffolding protocol for transparent and rigorous framework-based reviews in the social sciences
ideas.description: This approach creates a two-stage review protocol that first requires reviewers to explicitly articulate and document their epistemological orientation, the role of theory in evidence synthesis, and their criteria for evaluating the validity and relevance of diverse methodological contributions. This foundational 'scaffolding' stage ensures the subsequent application of structured reporting elements (adapted from PRISMA) is context-sensitive and philosophically coherent. The resulting system produces reviews that are both rigorously reported and epistemologically transparent, bridging the gap between procedural standardization and the substantive diversity of social science inquiry. It is designed for adoption by journals and scholarly communities to enhance the credibility and utility of framework-based reviews.

ideas.title: Incentivized Stewardship and Automated Decay Monitoring for Reliable Living Reviews
ideas.description: This research tests a stewardship-based framework to ensure the long-term reliability of living reviews in fast-moving fields. It proposes treating a living review as a versioned, citable research product with a formally recognized and incentivized steward responsible for its maintenance. The system employs automated alerts based on literature influx and citation dissonance to prompt necessary updates, and includes a formal deprecation or archiving mechanism for reviews that become inactive. The study will compare the accuracy, utility, and survival rates of reviews under this stewarded model against both classic static reviews and traditional, un-stewarded living reviews.
